ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai-style soft shell crab. FOODON_03302757 (crab food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW (Crab, cooked) is the closest available code. No specific code exists for soft shell crab or Thai-style preparation in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226766009 (Crab, food) is the closest available concept. No specific concept for soft shell crab or Thai-style preparation exists.

serving.description: 1 portion (approximately 1–2 whole soft shell crabs with sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Thai Style Soft Shell Crab consists of one to two whole fried soft shell crabs (approximately 200 g total including sauce), served as a main dish or shared plate.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with deep-frying and batter adjustment using USDA FoodData Central fried seafood coating data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Thai-style soft shell crab exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked blue crab (FDC ID 171975) was used as the base protein and mineral profile. Fat and energy values were adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying in batter (approximately 8–10 g additional fat per 100 g from frying, consistent with USDA fried seafood coating data). Carbohydrate contribution from batter and Thai chili sauce estimated from USDA data for fried battered seafood. Sodium adjusted to reflect fish sauce and chili sauce seasoning typical of Thai-style preparations. Values represent a best-estimate composite for the as-served dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on battered fried seafood and mixed Thai sauce components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style soft shell crab. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous fried battered seafood dishes with mixed sauce components. Battered fried fish and seafood products typically fall in the GI range of 38–55 (low category) due to the protein and fat content moderating glucose response. Thai chili sauce contributes a small carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (45 × 11.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 10.1 g. Source: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for fried seafood.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, chili sauce, and seasoning (approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1040 mg per typical 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Thai Style Soft Shell Crab due to its high sodium content (~1040 mg per serving, representing approximately 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg), primarily contributed by fish sauce and Thai chili sauce seasoning; portion reduction and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from batter and Thai chili sauce with low-to-moderate glycemic index (~45); high fat content from deep-frying may contribute to insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Thai Style Soft Shell Crab in controlled portions, as the estimated GI of 45 (low category) and moderate glycemic load (~10 per serving) are manageable, but the high fat content from deep-frying (~12.8 g fat per 100 g) and caloric density (~218 kcal per 100 g) warrant portion control and infrequent consumption to avoid excess caloric intake and potential worsening of insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density; moderate cholesterol content (~78 mg per 100 g); high sodium from seasoning sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Thai Style Soft Shell Crab sparingly, as deep-frying significantly increases total fat content and caloric density, the sodium level (~520 mg per 100 g) poses a risk for blood pressure elevation, and the cholesterol content (~78 mg per 100 g) contributes to dietary cholesterol load; however, the omega-3 fatty acids naturally present in crab provide some cardiovascular benefit when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Style Soft Shell Crab is a popular seafood dish served in Malaysian tze char restaurants and Southeast Asian eateries, featuring whole soft shell crabs that have been coated in a light seasoned batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then served with or tossed in a zesty Thai-style chili sauce made from fresh chilies, garlic, lemongrass, fish sauce, and lime juice. Soft shell crabs are blue crabs harvested immediately after moulting, when their entire shell is edible and tender, making the whole crab consumable including legs, body, and shell. The dish delivers a satisfying contrast of textures — a crunchy, airy fried exterior giving way to sweet, delicate crab meat — with the Thai chili sauce adding bright, spicy, sour, and umami flavour notes. Nutritionally, the dish provides a meaningful source of protein (~15.5 g per 100 g), is rich in vitamin B12 (~3.2 mcg per 100 g), zinc (~3.5 mg per 100 g), and selenium (~36 mcg per 100 g), and contains moderate cholesterol (~78 mg per 100 g). Deep-frying substantially increases the fat and caloric content compared to plain steamed crab. The high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) from fish sauce and chili sauce seasoning is a key clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 places this dish in the low GI category, making it relatively manageable for blood glucose control when consumed in appropriate portions. This dish is commonly found in Malaysian tze char restaurants, Singaporean seafood restaurants, and Thai-influenced eateries across Southeast Asia, and is typically ordered as a shared main course.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Thai-style soft shell crab exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked blue crab data with adjustments for deep-frying and sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate based on fried battered seafood entries from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked blue crab, FDC ID 171975) with upward fat and energy adjustments for deep-frying in batter and Thai chili sauce contribution. No exact match was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy crab entry and is not reliably quantified for this preparation. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Thai-style soft shell crab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. Halal status is marked false as the dish typically contains fish sauce (fermented fish product) and the halal certification of the specific restaurant preparation is unknown; consumers should verify with the restaurant. 5. The dish is served at George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer (Boat Quay, Singapore) at SGD 28.00 per portion. 6. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and Thai chili sauce; this is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. 7. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not reported in the adapted USDA source for this preparation: vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked blue crab data adapted for deep-frying and Thai chili sauce.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg) with explicit reason in notes.
Estimated GI at 45 using analogous fried battered seafood entries from University of Sydney GI Database; documented estimation basis.
Provided multilingual local names and aliases for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai as required.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values (closest_parent) for all three ontology systems.
Confirmed gi_category = 'low' is correct for GI = 45 (≤55 threshold).
Included specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions with concern levels and key factors.
